name: Buck / Buck2
summary: Meta's open-sourced build system descended from Google's Blaze; Buck2 (2023+, Rust rewrite of the original Java/Python Buck) is the current production version with per-target visibility, distributed remote-execution support, and BUCK files declaring build targets.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: **Buck** (Meta's open-sourced build system) implements per-target visibility declarations like Bazel (`data/bazel`): each target in a BUCK file has a `visibility` attribute restricting which other targets can depend on it. **Buck2** (2023+) is the Rust rewrite of the original Buck (Java/Python) and is the current production version at Meta.

sections.title: Buck2 — Rust rewrite and remote execution
sections.content:
Buck2 was open-sourced in 2023 as a complete Rust rewrite of the original Buck. The architectural emphasis is on:

- **Distributed remote-execution** as a first-class concern — a single Buck2 invocation can dispatch build actions across a cluster, with content-addressed action caching per shipped artifact.
- **Speed and incrementality** through fine-grained dependency tracking. Buck2 reports significant build-time improvements over Buck1 on large monorepos.
- **Starlark-based BUCK files** — same configuration language as Bazel, ensuring some interoperability of build-rule patterns between the two ecosystems.

Production: Meta uses Buck2 internally for the entire monorepo; external adopters have been growing since the open-source release, though Bazel's broader ecosystem reach continues to dominate the public market.

sections.title: Positioning vs Bazel
sections.content:
Distinct from Bazel: both descend from Google's internal Blaze design vocabulary, both use Starlark for BUILD/BUCK files, both implement per-target visibility. The differences are largely organisational and incremental:

- Buck2 is Rust-based with very fast incremental rebuilds; Bazel is Java-based with a larger plugin ecosystem.
- Buck2's remote-execution model is somewhat tighter than Bazel's (designed-in rather than added-on).
- Bazel has a larger external ecosystem (BCR, more rule sets, broader language support); Buck2 is newer in the open-source space.

The lesson generalises with Bazel's: **at sufficient scale, build-system visibility becomes the dominant abstraction** regardless of which build system enforces it.
